attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic at 36 weeks and four days gestation. This is her first pregnancy and has been uncomplicated until now. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. At her last three visits over the past two weeks, the fetus has been documented in different positions: breech, then cephalic, and transverse. Three bedside ultrasound scans over this period have confirmed these changing positions.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, and temperature 36.8°C. The fetus is currently palpated in a breech position. The estimated fetal weight on her 36 week ultrasound scan is appropriate for gestational age.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a fetus that has been documented in multiple different positions over a short period after 36 weeks gestation. This is characteristic of unstable lie, which occurs when the fetus frequently changes position between longitudinal and transverse lies in late pregnancy. This condition requires hospital admission due to the risk of cord prolapse if membrane rupture occurs when the fetus is in a transverse position.

Frank breech presentation describes a fetus in a bottom-first position with both hips flexed and both knees extended. While this is a common type of breech presentation, it is typically stable and would not change position frequently as described in this case.

Complete breech presentation occurs when the fetus is bottom-first with both hips and knees flexed. Like frank breech, this presentation tends to be stable and would not account for the frequently changing positions observed here.

Oblique lie describes a fetal position where the long axis of the fetus is at an angle to the mother's longitudinal axis. While this can occur transiently, it typically resolves into either a longitudinal or transverse lie and would not account for the multiple documented position changes.

Compound presentation occurs when a fetal extremity prolapses alongside the presenting part, typically during labour. This is not relevant to the antenatal finding of changing fetal positions described in this case.

The management of unstable lie after 36 weeks includes hospital admission for close monitoring, as spontaneous rupture of membranes with the fetus in a transverse position can lead to cord prolapse, which is an obstetric emergency. Elective caesarean section is typically recommended if the lie remains unstable as term approaches.
learningPoints: Unstable lie after 36 weeks gestation requires hospital admission due to risk of cord prolapse.
Fetal position that changes frequently between longitudinal and transverse lies after 36 weeks is diagnostic of unstable lie.
Serial ultrasound examinations are essential to document changing fetal positions in suspected unstable lie.
references: SA Health. Unstable lie of the fetus: Clinical practice guideline [Internet]. Adelaide: Government of South Australia; 2021 [cited 2024 Nov 22]. Available from: https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/249e33804eee75fdbef8bf6a7ac0d6e4/Unstable+lie+of+the+fetus_PPG_v4_1.pdf?MOD=AJPERES&CACHEID=ROOTWORKSPACE-249e33804eee75fdbef8bf6a7ac0d6e4-p4cjEdZ

Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG). Umbilical cord prolapse: Green-top guideline no. 50 [Internet]. London: RCOG; 2014 [cited 2024 Nov 22]. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/media/3wykswng/gtg-50-umbilicalcordprolapse-2014.pdf
